q:
There is a traffic jam. When must an emergency corridor be formed?

A. Only on motorways when emergency services are approaching.
B. Always on all roads with at least 2 lanes in the same direction.
e:
LESSON 4
In case of a traffic jam, drivers on a carriageway with two or more lanes in their direction must preventively form an emergency corridor for emergency services. This must be done before traffic comes to a complete stop.
On a two-lane road: between the left and right lane.
On a three-lane road: between the left and middle lane.
Drivers in the right lane may not move onto the hard shoulder, the bus lane, or any special lane reserved for buses.
clean_q:

A. Only on motorways when emergency services are approaching.
B. Always on all roads with at least 2 lanes in the same direction.
clean_e:
LESSON 4
In case of a traffic jam, drivers on a carriageway with two or more lanes in their direction must preventively form an emergency corridor for emergency services. This must be done before traffic comes to a complete stop.
On a two-lane road: between the left and right lane.
On a three-lane road: between the left and middle lane.
Drivers in the right lane may not move onto the hard shoulder, the bus lane, or any special lane reserved for buses.

q:
The Biological Clock
During which hours does the combination of fatigue, reduced cognitive performance, and physiological fluctuations make the body more vulnerable, potentially leading to an increase in accidents?

A. Between 11:00 p.m. and 2:00 a.m.
B. Between 2:00 a.m. and 5:00 a.m.
C. Between 5:00 a.m. and 8:00 a.m.
e:
Explanation:
Research shows that accident frequency rises during the night’s low hours, especially between 2:00 and 5:00 a.m. This is because the body’s circadian rhythm affects alertness, concentration, and reaction times. During this time window, fatigue and physiological weakness are at their peak, leading to slower responses and a higher likelihood of mistakes.

C. Between 5:00 a.m. and 8:00 a.m.

q:
What should you do after starting the car engine?

A. Drive off immediately.
B. Wait about 30 seconds.
C. Wait until the engine is fully warm.
e:
LESSON 30
To reduce environmental impact and engine wear, you should let the engine run for about 30 seconds after starting, so that the oil circulates properly. Then drive gently for the first few kilometers. Letting the engine idle for a long time wastes fuel and can harm lubrication. Drive moderately until the engine reaches its normal operating temperature.

q:
It is snowing heavily in Wallonia and the visibility is 80 metres.
Which lights must be switched on?

A. Dipped headlights and the front and rear fog lights.
B. Only the dipped headlights.
C. Only the dipped headlights and the rear fog light.
e:
LESSON 11
Front fog lights are not mandatory on a car. Therefore, they are never compulsory to use.
A car must however be equipped with one or two red rear fog lights, which must be switched on:
- in fog, when visibility is less than approximately 100 metres;
- in snowfall, when visibility is less than approximately 100 metres;
- in heavy rain, they must always be switched on in Belgium.
The dipped headlights must be switched on during the day when visibility is less than 200 metres.

q:
From what alcohol level in the breath are you committing an offence?

A. From 0.22 mg/l.
B. From 0.25 mg/l.
C. From 0.35 mg/l.
e:
LESSON 28
- If you have less than 0.22 mg/l, you may continue driving.
- If the result is 0.22 mg/l or more, but below 0.35 mg/l, you must hand over your driving licence for 3 hours.
It is an offence and you will receive a fine.
- If you have 0.35 mg/l or more, you must hand over your driving licence for 6 hours.
An immediate withdrawal of your licence is also possible, and you will receive a fine.
After those 6 hours, a new breath test or breath analysis will follow.

q:
Does the priority to the right also apply in a supermarket car park?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 20
The priority to the right also generally applies in a supermarket car park, as it is usually considered a place accessible to public traffic.
Therefore, the normal road traffic rules apply.
Important
Leaving a parking space (forwards or backwards) is a special manoeuvre: the driver must give way to all other traffic.
If there are road signs, yield markings, stop signs or arrows on the ground, these priority rules must be followed.

q:
The judge is obliged to impose a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle for speeding in a built-up area by more than …

A. 20 km/h.
B. 30 km/h.
C. 40 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
If you exceed the maximum speed limit by more than 40 km/h on a motorway, a road for motor vehicles, or an ordinary road, you risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
In addition, if you exceed the maximum speed by more than 30 km/h in a built-up area, in a 30-zone, or in a residential area, you also risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
This is not a decision taken by the police, but by a judge in court, when your case is examined.

q:
At which intersection are you allowed to overtake this two-wheeled motorcycle on the left?

A. Only after sign 1.
B. Only after signs 1 and 2.
C. After all these signs.
e:
LESSON 19
Sign 1 = priority at the next intersection.
Sign 2 = priority road.
Sign 3 = priority from the right.
At intersections where you have the right of way, you may overtake all vehicles on the left.

q:
A tyre has four DOT digits (xxxx).
These digits indicate when the tyre was manufactured. What do they mean?

A. The first two digits indicate the year and the last two the week.
B. The first two digits indicate the week and the last two the year.
e:
LESSON 31
The DOT number (Department of Transportation) on a tyre indicates the manufacturing date.
It consists of 4 digits:
- the first two digits indicate the week of production;
- the last two digits indicate the year of production.

q:
This roadway is divided into two lanes.
What is the maximum speed allowed in the Brussels Region after passing the yellow sign?

A. 60 km/h.
B. 70 km/h.
C. 90 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
It is a normal roadway divided into two lanes.
In the Walloon Region, the maximum permitted speed is 90 km/h.
In the Flemish and Brussels Regions, it is 70 km/h.
The yellow sign has nothing to do with speed — it is a priority-road sign indicating that you are driving on a priority road.

q:
May a child shorter than 1.35 metres sit in the front seat of the car in a special child seat, as shown here?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 10
Children under 18 years old and shorter than 1.35 m must be transported in an appropriate child-safety seat or restraint system that complies with legal standards.
A child taller than 1.35 m may still use a child seat if desired.
Children under 18 may not sit in the front in a rear-facing child seat, unless there is no front airbag or it can be deactivated.

q:
You park your car at 15:06 in a street where you must use a parking disc.
At what time do you set the arrow?

A. At 15:00.
B. At 15:15.
C. At 15:30.
e:
LESSON 27
On the parking disc, you set the arrow to the mark that follows your arrival time. The mark after 15:06 is 15:30.
You may then park for two hours, counted from the time shown by the arrow, unless a traffic sign specifies a different duration.
After that, you must move your car — you may not simply change the time on the parking disc.

q:
When is a driver, when it is fairly dark outside built-up areas, allowed to keep the high beams on?

A. When an oncoming vehicle is approaching.
B. When another car is driving in front of him.
C. When overtaking the car in front of him.
e:
LESSON 11
Article 30/1
High beams must be switched off and replaced by low beams:
a) when approaching an oncoming road user,
b) when approaching a rail vehicle or a boat whose driver could be dazzled,
c) when following another vehicle at less than 50 meters, except when overtaking,
d) when the road is continuously and sufficiently lit so the driver can clearly see up to about 100 meters.

Unnecessary idling or driving at high engine speed increases pollution and fuel consumption.

q:
You are involved in an accident.
May the police ask you to take a saliva test?

A. Only if there is a strong suspicion of drug use.
B. Yes, in the event of an accident, always.
e:
LESSON 28
Normally, a saliva test may only be carried out if the police suspect drug use.
BUT NOTE: if you are involved in an accident, the police may always require a saliva test.
If the result is above the permitted limit:
- your driving licence must be immediately surrendered for 12 hours;
- an immediate withdrawal of the driving licence is also possible;
- after the 12 hours, a new saliva test is carried out.
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving ban of up to 5 years.

q:
You drive past this traffic sign with your car.
Are you allowed to overtake a motorcycle with a sidecar on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
The red prohibition sign indicates that drivers are not allowed to overtake an articulated vehicle or any vehicle with more than two wheels on the left. This no-overtaking rule applies until the next intersection, or it ends earlier if you see the END sign.
Therefore, a car driver may overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle on the left, but not a motorcycle with a sidecar.
Conversely, a motorcyclist may not overtake a car, since a car has more than two wheels.

q:
Which answer is WRONG?

A. The car may use studded tyres.
B. The car may use snow chains.
C. The front and rear fog lights must be switched on.
e:
LESSON 31
The question was: “which answer is wrong.”
A. Correct. Studded tyres are allowed between 1 November and 31 March.
B. Correct. Snow chains may always be used when there is snow on the roadway.
C. Wrong. Only the rear fog lights must be switched on.

B. The car turning left.

LESSON 21

A driver coming from a dirt road must give way to traffic on the paved road, even when driving straight ahead.

q:
The ladder extends 1 meter in front of the car body.
Is the driver allowed to drive like this?

A. Yes, because it is a maximum of 1 meter.
B. Yes, if he signals the protruding load.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 10
A load may never extend beyond the front of the car body. At the rear, it may extend a maximum of 1 meter.
If you need to transport a load that is longer and indivisible or cannot be collapsed or folded, it may extend up to 3 meters behind the car body, provided that you attach this warning panel to the rear of the load.

q:
From which speeding offence in a zone 30 may the police immediately withdraw the driving licence?
A. Driving more than 20 km/h over the limit.
B. Driving more than 30 km/h over the limit.
C. Driving more than 40 km/h over the limit.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence:
- when you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, express roads and regular roads;
- but in built-up areas, zone 30 areas or residential zones, already when you exceed the limit by more than 20 km/h.
This immediate withdrawal is carried out by the police, because it concerns a serious offence.
For any serious offence, the driving licence may be withdrawn immediately.

B: A saliva test.
C: An alcohol test and a saliva test.
e:

In case of an accident, a saliva test is always allowed.

q:
The blue car is entering the intersection.
Is the driver allowed to do that?
e:
LESSON 20
At intersections where: there is no authorized officer, no traffic lights, and no signs regulating priority, the rule of priority to the right applies. This means that every driver (of a car, motorcycle, two-wheeled moped, or bicycle) must yield to a driver coming from the right, except when driving on a roundabout or when the driver coming from the right is coming from a prohibited direction.

q:

You want to enter the motorway.
Which statement is correct?

A. You must drive to the very end of the acceleration lane.
B. You must give way.
C. You have priority when merging.
e:
LESSON 4
When you enter a motorway via an acceleration lane,
you must give way to traffic already driving on the motorway.
That is why there is a yield (inverted triangle) road sign.
You are not required to drive to the end of the acceleration lane.
You may merge as soon as you have enough speed and it can be done safely.

q:
You are driving at 105 km/h on an ordinary road in Flanders.
What is the consequence?

A. A fine.
B. An immediate withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear in court.
e:
LESSON 12
The maximum permitted speed on an ordinary road in Flanders is 70 km/h. You are therefore driving 35 km/h too fast.
1. Immediate temporary withdrawal of the driving licence.
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your licence as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, expressways, and ordinary roads.
2. Disqualification from the right to drive a motor vehicle (judge).
-You risk disqualification as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on those same roads.

q:
The cyclist and the truck both want to turn right at this intersection.
How does the cyclist react?

A. He hurries so that he can turn first.
B. He turns at the same time as the truck.
C. He slows down and lets the truck turn first.
e:
LESSON 10
No matter how well the mirrors are adjusted, there are always areas beside or behind the vehicle that the driver of a truck or car cannot see.
These areas are called blind spots.
Many accidents happen at intersections because a cyclist is in the truck’s blind spot.
A smart cyclist slows down and lets the truck turn first.

q:
May the truck overtake the blue car on the left?

A. Yes, because a truck is not a passenger car.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 16
The red circular prohibition sign indicates that it is forbidden for drivers to:
- overtake a combination of vehicles, or
- overtake any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle,
but the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

q:
Traffic is moving very slowly and keeps stopping.
If traffic comes to a halt again, are you allowed to stop on an elevated section?

A. Yes.
B. No, you must stop before the elevated section.
e:
LESSON 25
You are not allowed to stop or park on an elevated section, unless road markings or traffic signs indicate otherwise.
This rule is strict.
⚠️ However: in a traffic jam, you may stop briefly to follow the flow of traffic.
This is not considered illegal stopping, but a stop imposed by traffic conditions.

q:
The ABS warning light stays on. What should you do?

A. Remove the fuse so that the warning light goes out.
B. Continue driving, but go to a garage as soon as possible.
C. You must stop and are not allowed to continue driving until it is repaired by a mechanic.
e:
LESSON 31
The ABS operates when you press the brake pedal hard and deeply.
If the ABS warning light remains on, the ABS system no longer works, but the normal braking system still functions.
You may continue driving, but you should go to a garage as soon as possible.

q:
The police stop you in a built-up area in Flanders because you were driving at 75 km/h. What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear before the court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
But in built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already risk this when you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a driving disqualification as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is decided by the judge, not by the police.

C. You will have to appear before the court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12

You risk a driving disqualification as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In built-up areas, 30 km/h zones, or residential areas, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is decided by the judge, not by the police.

q:
What happens if you refuse to take a saliva test?

A. You receive a temporary driving ban of 6 hours.
B. You receive a temporary driving ban of 10 hours.
C. You receive a temporary driving ban of 12 hours.
e:
LESSON 28
A saliva test may only be administered when the police suspect drug use.
Although Belgian law allows you to refuse the test, it is strongly advised to cooperate. If you refuse, the police must call a doctor to evaluate your reasons.
If the doctor concludes that you have no legal grounds for refusing, you must pay the doctor’s costs and you will receive a temporary 12-hour driving ban.
During a saliva test, a swab is used to collect saliva from your mouth to determine the presence of drugs.
If the result exceeds the permitted value:
– you must immediately surrender your licence for 12 hours
– an immediate withdrawal of the licence is possible
– after 12 hours, a new saliva test is required
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving-ban sentence of up to 5 years.

During a saliva test, a swab is used to collect saliva from your mouth to determine the presence of drugs.
If the result exceeds the permitted value:
– you must immediately surrender your licence for 12 hours
– an immediate withdrawal of the licence is possible
– after 12 hours, a new saliva test is required
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving-ban sentence of up to 5 years.

q:
This warning sign indicates a pedestrian crossing.
Where is this sign placed in Flanders?

A. Close to the pedestrian crossing.
B. 100 metres before the pedestrian crossing.
C. 150 metres before the pedestrian crossing.
e:
LESSON 7
In Flanders, warning signs are generally placed 150 metres before the hazard.
This allows the driver to be warned in time, so they can reduce speed and pay extra attention.
In practice, this distance may be adjusted (for example in built-up areas or when visibility is limited), but the general rule remains: 150 metres in advance.

q: If the air filter is dirty, does it increase fuel consumption?
e:
LESSON 31
A clogged or dirty air filter reduces the airflow to the engine, which increases the amount of fuel required to maintain vehicle performance.
A dirty air filter therefore has a significant impact on fuel consumption, whether the vehicle runs on petrol or diesel.
Depending on your vehicle and fuel type, the increase in consumption can range from 10% to 25%.

LESSON 6

In Wallonia the speed limit is 90 km/h.

q:
A roadway not divided into lanes inside a built-up area.
May the red car overtake the white car on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
This prohibition sign indicates that trucks or combinations with a maximum authorised mass exceeding 3500 kg may not overtake:
- a combination of vehicles, or
- any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a truck on the left after this sign.
A bus may also overtake, since it is not a truck.

q:
Can it be dangerous to always use cruise control?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 12
Cruise control is a feature found in most modern vehicles. It helps promote safer and more economical driving by reducing fuel consumption.
Once activated, it maintains a constant speed without the driver needing to press the accelerator.
However, it does not take poor road conditions into account.
In cases of snow, ice, or slippery surfaces, the car’s wheels can lose grip while cruise control continues to maintain the preset speed. This can cause loss of control or skidding, especially if the driver brakes suddenly to deactivate the system.
In such conditions, experts strongly recommend not using cruise control to maintain full control of the vehicle

q:
What is the maximum permitted speed for a car with studded tyres on a road with two carriageways, each having two lanes, separated by a central reservation?

A. 70 km/h
B. 90 km/h
C. 120 km/h
e:
LESSON 5
Studded tyres may be used between 1 November and 31 March on vehicles with a maximum authorised mass up to 3.5 tons.

q:
This girl is 15 years old. She is cycling on the roadway within a built-up area.
May she also ride on the raised pavement?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 3
If there is a cycle path, a cyclist must use it (unless it is not rideable).
It is forbidden to cycle on the pavement within a built-up area, except for children under 10, who may always ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.
Outside built-up areas, cyclists may, if there is no usable cycle path and provided they ride on the right-hand side of the road, ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.

q:
The minimum tread depth of a car tyre must be:

A. 1.6 mm.
B. 1.9 mm.
C. 2.3 mm.
e:
LESSON 31
The tread depth of the main grooves of a tyre must be at least 1.6 mm.
However, at this legal minimum, driving is no longer truly safe:
with a tread depth of less than 2 mm, the risk of aquaplaning increases significantly.
Many tyres are equipped with wear indicators that show when replacement is necessary.
You should therefore check tyre condition regularly.

q:
A moving truck is blocking the carriageway.
May the car drive over the special traversable lane to pass the truck?

A. No, the car may not cross the solid white line.
B. Yes, but the car must give way to the tram.
e:
LESSON 22
A special traversable lane is not part of the carriageway.
With a car, you are therefore not allowed to:
- drive on it;
- stop on it;
- park on it.
It is also not allowed, in the last metres before an intersection, to use a special traversable lane to position yourself for turning left or right.
The solid white line marking the special traversable lane is not the same as a solid white line separating traffic lanes.
A car may cross a special traversable lane:
- to reach a property, a parking area or a petrol station;
- or to go around an obstacle.
A chequerboard marking indicates where drivers may cross the special traversable lane at intersections.
➡️ When crossing it, the driver must always give way to the tram.

q:
This DHL driver is delivering parcels.
Does he have to wear a seat belt while driving?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 10
In principle, a driver must always wear a seat belt.
However, the law provides a few strict exceptions.
The following persons are exempt from wearing a seat belt:
- a driver who is reversing;
- taxi drivers while transporting passengers;
- postal workers delivering parcels from house to house
(this does not apply to delivery drivers such as DHL, Amazon or other courier services);
- authorised persons and emergency services, when required for their duties.
➡️ A DHL driver is not exempt and must therefore wear a seat belt.

q:
Must you use your indicator when you enter the motorway via the acceleration lane?

A. Yes.
B. No, because you can only drive in that direction.
e:
LESSON 4
What does Article 13 of the traffic regulations say?
Before carrying out a manoeuvre or a movement that requires a lateral displacement or a change of direction, the driver must indicate their intention in good time by using the direction indicators, if the vehicle is equipped with them, or otherwise, if possible, by a hand signal. This indication must stop as soon as the lateral movement or change of direction has been completed.

Before carrying out a manoeuvre or a movement that requires a lateral displacement or a change of direction, the driver must indicate their intention in good time by using the direction indicators, if the vehicle is equipped with them, or otherwise, if possible, by a hand signal. This indication must stop as soon as the lateral movement or change of direction has been completed.

B. You notify the police.
C. You leave the cars where they are, and fill in the European accident report.

If that is truly impossible and the cars block the intersection, then you notify the police.

q:
A doctor decides that you are medically unfit to drive a vehicle.
Within how many days must you hand in your driving licence at the municipality?

A. The next day.
B. Within 4 days.
C. Within one week.
e:
LESSON – Introduction driving licence B
If a doctor decides that you are physically or mentally unfit to drive, you must inform the municipality and surrender your driving licence within 4 days after receiving this decision.
Saturdays, Sundays and public holidays are not included.

B. Stop only.
C. Neither stop nor park.

LESSON 26

If there is a verge, parking there is allowed.
➡️ You must therefore park on the opposite side of the road.

q:
The judge is obliged to impose a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle for speeding on an ordinary road by more than …

A. 20 km/h.
B. 30 km/h.
C. 40 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
If you exceed the maximum speed limit by more than 40 km/h on a motorway, a road for motor vehicles, or an ordinary road, you risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
In addition, if you exceed the maximum speed by more than 30 km/h in a built-up area, in a 30-zone, or in a residential area, you also risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
This is not a decision taken by the police, but by a judge in court, when your case is examined.

q: Sometimes a police officer stands at a junction with one arm stretched out horizontally. Sometimes the officer stretches out both arms horizontally. Does this give exactly the same instruction?
e:
LESSON 17
When a police officer holds one or both arms stretched out horizontally, road users who are approaching the officer’s chest or back must stop.
Road users who are approaching the officer’s sides or fingertips may enter the junction in order to:
• go straight ahead,
• turn left,
• or turn right.

q:
You are driving at 120 km/h on a motorway and it starts raining heavily.
What do you do?

A. Keep a safe distance of 60 metres.
B. Slow down and increase the distance.
e:
LESSON 13
The faster you drive, the more distance you must keep.
A simple formula helps you calculate it: speed divided by two.
At 120 km/h, the safe following distance is 60 metres.
At 100 km/h, it becomes 50 metres.
But in heavy rain, snow or fog, you must slow down and increase the following distance.

q:
On the dashboard of many cars, there is a switch labelled cruise or cruise control.
What happens when you press this switch?

A. It prevents you from driving faster than the maximum permitted speed.
B. It ensures that you keep driving at the same speed.
e:
LESSON 12
Cruise control is used to automatically maintain a constant, preset speed without the driver having to keep the accelerator pedal pressed.
It improves driving comfort and reduces driver fatigue, especially during long journeys on motorways.
⚠️ Important: cruise control is not a speed limiter.
It does not prevent you from exceeding the maximum permitted speed.

- reverse.

q:
The saliva test is positive.
How many hours of temporary driving ban do you immediately receive?

A. 6 hours.
B. 12 hours.
C. 24 hours.
e:
LESSON 28
During a saliva test, a cotton swab is used to scrape saliva from the mouth to determine the presence of drugs.
If the result exceeds the legal limit: you must immediately hand over your driving licence for 12 hours. An immediate withdrawal of the licence is also possible. After 12 hours, a new saliva test follows.
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving ban of up to 5 years.
Refusing the saliva test = immediate 12-hour driving ban.
The saliva test may only be carried out if the police suspect drug use.

Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving ban of up to 5 years.
Refusing the saliva test = immediate 12-hour driving ban.
The saliva test may only be carried out if the police suspect drug use.

q:
The driver of the white car realises that he is driving in the wrong direction.
May he turn around?

A. Yes, because there is a broken white line.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 31
In a tunnel, you must never turn around or reverse, regardless of the road markings.
This happens more often than you might think, for example in traffic jams, incidents or vehicle breakdowns, and leads to life-threatening situations.
In a tunnel, always be aware of emergency exits, emergency call points and fire extinguishers.

q:
May a driver hold his mobile phone to read a message while passengers are getting into the car?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON
You may use the device without a holder only if your vehicle is stationary or correctly parked.
The term “stationary” must be interpreted as defined in the Belgian Road Code:
“remaining stationary only for the time strictly necessary to allow passengers to get in or out of the vehicle, or to load or unload the vehicle.”
Briefly stopping at a traffic light, for example, is not considered ‘being stationary’ within the meaning of the Road Code.

q:
You are approaching an intersection where an officer is standing with one arm extended horizontally.
What instruction is the officer giving?

A. You must turn right.
B. You may continue driving, but not turn right.
C. You must stop.
e:
LESSON 17
When an officer extends one or both arms horizontally, road users approaching the officer’s chest or back must stop.
Road users approaching the officer’s fingertips or side may continue onto the intersection to go straight, turn left, or turn right.
An officer with both arms extended horizontally gives exactly the same instruction as with only one arm extended horizontally.

q:
A one-way street within a built-up area.
May the blue car continue driving on this lane?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 15
Within a built-up area, drivers may choose the lane that best suits their destination, provided that:
- the road is divided into lanes, and
- the road is located in a built-up area.
This is allowed in two cases:
- on one-way roads with lanes;
- on two-way roads divided into four or more lanes, with at least two lanes for each direction.

q:
It is night. A driver is overtaking another car.
May the high beams be on during the overtaking?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 11
High beams must not be used:
- when the road is continuously and sufficiently lit so that you can see 100 metres ahead;
- when they would dazzle an oncoming road user;
- when a vehicle is driving less than 50 metres ahead of you and you are not overtaking it immediately. If you are overtaking, you may use them.
- when approaching a rail vehicle or a boat.

q:
What is the maximum speed allowed in the Flemish Region on an ordinary road when using studded tyres?

A. 60 km/h.
B. 70 km/h.
C. 90 km/h.
e:
LESSON 4
Studded tyres may be used from 1 November to 31 March on vehicles with a maximum authorised mass of up to 3.5 tonnes.
The maximum permitted speed is:
- on motorways and roads with 2×2 lanes separated by a central strip: 90 km/h;
- on ordinary roads in all regions: 60 km/h.

B. No. It is a fourth-degree offence.
e:
LESSON 18
You are not allowed to enter a level crossing when:
• an audible warning signal is sounding,
• the red warning lights are flashing,
• or the barriers are lowering.
⚠️ Note:
Only when the barriers are raised, the red lights are off, the white moonlight is on, and no audible signal is heard, overtaking other vehicles on a level crossing is permitted.

q:
May the truck overtake the blue car on the left?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 16
The overtaking prohibition begins within 200 metres.
The red circular prohibition sign indicates that it is forbidden for drivers to:
- overtake a combination of vehicles, or
- overtake any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle,
but the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

q:
Nighttime, outside a built-up area.
Before starting to overtake, the driver of the blue car briefly used the horn.
Was he allowed to do that?

A. No, because it is night.
B. Yes, because it is outside the built-up area.
e:
LESSON 11
When may you use the horn?
- During the day:
Inside and outside built-up areas: to warn others and prevent an accident.
Outside built-up areas: to warn a driver you intend to overtake.
- Between dusk and dawn:
Only in case of imminent danger.
In all other cases, you must flash your headlights alternately instead of using the horn.

Only in case of imminent danger.
In all other cases, you must flash your headlights alternately instead of using the horn.

A Belgian driving licence in bank card format is valid for 10 years. After 10 years, you must renew it at your local municipality, without having to retake the exam.

q:
The judge is obliged to impose a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle for speeding on a motorway by more than …

A. 20 km/h.
B. 30 km/h.
C. 40 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
If you exceed the maximum speed limit by more than 40 km/h on a motorway, a road for motor vehicles, or an ordinary road, you risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
In addition, if you exceed the maximum speed by more than 30 km/h in a built-up area, in a 30-zone, or in a residential area, you also risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
This is not a decision taken by the police, but by a judge in court, when your case is examined.

q:
You are driving at 75 km/h within a built-up area in Flanders.
What are the consequences?

A. A fine.
B. An immediate withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear in court.
e:
LESSON 12
1. If you drive in a built-up area, a 30-zone, or a residential area at more than 20 km/h above the speed limit, you risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence. This withdrawal is carried out by the police, because it is considered a serious offence. For any serious offence, your licence can be taken away on the spot.
2. In a built-up area, a 30-zone, or a residential area, if you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h, you risk a driving ban pronounced by the judge in court. Depending on the ruling, you may be prohibited from driving any motor vehicle (even those not requiring a licence) for a period ranging from at least 8 days up to 5 years. For novice drivers (< 2 years with a full category B licence), the rules are even stricter.

q:
What is the reaction distance when you are driving at 90 km/h?

A. 17 metres.
B. 22 metres.
C. 27 metres.
e:
LESSON 13
Between the moment the driver sees the danger and the moment the brain processes this information and realises that the brake pedal must be pressed, the vehicle has already travelled a certain distance.
The higher the speed, the longer the distance covered during the reaction time.
• At 50 km/h, the reaction distance is about 15 metres.
• At 90 km/h, the reaction distance is about 27 metres.
• At 120 km/h, the reaction distance is about 36 metres.
- This happens before the brake pedal is pressed.
- A drunk driver reacts more slowly, which makes the reaction distance even longer.

q:
May you overtake a slow-moving cyclist on the left in a 30 km/h zone?

A. No.
B. Yes, if you can keep a sufficient lateral distance.
e:
LESSON 6
A 30 km/h zone is not the same as a cycle zone.
Only in a cycle zone is it forbidden to overtake a cyclist.
In a 30 km/h zone, you may overtake a cyclist on the left provided you keep a sufficient lateral distance.

q:
During a police check, you realise that you forgot your wallet with your driving licence at home. What are the consequences?

A. You are not allowed to continue driving.
B. You only receive a first-degree fine.
C. You must show your driving licence at a police station within 5 days.
e:
INFO – DRIVING LICENCE
Not carrying your driving licence with you is not considered a serious offence. You may receive a small administrative fine, but you are generally allowed to continue driving.
However, you must present your licence at a police station within 5 days.
If your licence is lost or stolen, you may not drive until you receive a new one. A loss or theft certificate is not sufficient to allow you to drive.

q:
A driver is checked and has an alcohol level of 0.5 g/l of blood.
May he continue driving?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 28
In Belgium, a driver with an alcohol level of 0.5 g/l of blood (equivalent to 0.22 mg/l of breath alcohol) may not continue driving immediately.
He faces:
- a driving ban of at least 3 hours,
- a fine starting from €179,
- and a possible suspension or withdrawal of the driving licence in case of repeat offences or aggravating circumstances.

q:
The driver of this car saw no other road users and carefully entered the intersection without stopping.
Was he allowed to do that?

A. Yes, because there is no stop line on the road.
B. No, he must stop first.
e:
LESSON 19
A STOP means you must come to a complete stop at the stop line and yield to drivers on the public road you want to enter.
If there is no stop line on the pavement, you must stop at the traffic sign.
Even if there are no drivers on the crossing road, you must still come to a complete stop before entering the intersection.

q:
The windshield washer reservoir is empty. This may negatively affect the driver’s visibility.
What should you do?

A. Stop immediately and refill.
B. Drive to the nearest garage.
C. Refill as soon as possible.
e:
LESSON 31
If you hear a humming or buzzing sound when you activate the washer but no liquid sprays onto the windshield, the reservoir is empty. You should refill it as soon as possible, because without washer fluid your visibility can be greatly reduced, especially in rain or when dirt splashes up from the road. Just like engine oil, the washer fluid level should be checked regularly.

q:
Up to how many metres before a pedestrian crossing is it forbidden to park on the roadway?

A. 1 metre.
B. 5 metres.
C. 10 metres.
e:
LESSON 7
It is forbidden to park or stop on a pedestrian crossing, even to let someone get in or out.
It is also forbidden to park or to stop on the roadway within 5 metres before the crossing (parking on the verge is allowed if possible).
The same rule applies to crossings for cyclists and two-wheeled mopeds.
After the crossing, parking and stopping are allowed, unless prohibited by traffic signs.

A. The maximum permitted speed is 90 km/h.
B. The maximum permitted speed is 60 km/h.
C. He is no longer allowed to drive with studded tyres.
e:
LESSON 31
Studded tyres may only be used between 1 November and 31 March on vehicles with a maximum authorised mass of up to 3.5 tonnes.
Maximum permitted speeds:
- On ordinary roads: 60 km/h
- On motorways and roads with 2x2 lanes: 90 km/h
Snow chains may be used when there is snow or ice on the roadway.

q:
Which traffic sign is associated with the road marking called shark’s teeth?

A. With sign 1.
B. With sign 2.
C. With sign 3.
e:
An inverted triangle means you must give way to drivers on the road you are about to enter and stop if necessary.
If there are no drivers on the crossing road, you don’t have to stop and may enter the intersection.
A line formed by inverted triangles, also known as shark’s teeth, indicates the place where you must stop if necessary.
If it’s not really necessary, you don’t have to make a full stop.

q:
You damaged another car while parking. You don’t know who the owner is.
How much time do you have to report it at a police station?

A. 12 hours.
B. 24 hours.
C. As soon as possible.
e:
LESSON 29
If you damage someone’s property and the police cannot come to the scene, you must report it as soon as possible at a police station or online via Police-on-Web (for minor damage). The sooner you report it, the more likely it is that the facts will be correctly recorded. Make sure you have all necessary information, such as the location and time of the incident, the license plate of the damaged vehicle, and photos of the damage.

q:
It has snowed.
What must you do before driving your car?

A. Clean the windshield and mirrors.
B. Clean the mirrors and all windows.
C. Remove the snow from the entire car.
e: In Belgium, when it snows, you must clear the entire car. Start with the windows (windshield, rear window, side windows) and mirrors to ensure good visibility, then remove the snow from the roof, the hood, and the rest of the bodywork. This is both a safety and a legal requirement, since snow falling from your car can endanger other road users.

q:
The police stopped you because you were driving at 67 km/h in a built-up area in the Brussels region.
What are the consequences?

A. You only have to pay a fine.
B. Your driving licence will be temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to appear in court.
e:
LESSON 12
The maximum speed limit in a built-up area in the Brussels region is 30 km/h.
You were therefore driving 36 km/h too fast.
A driving disqualification is imposed when you exceed the limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already risk disqualification if you exceed the limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not done by the police but by a judge in court.
Depending on the verdict, you may lose your right to drive for at least 8 days and up to 5 years, even for vehicles that do not require a driving licence.
For novice drivers (with a full driving licence for less than 2 years), the rule is even stricter.

q:
At what maximum speed may this car drive, inside a built-up area in the Walloon Region, past this sign?

A. 30 km/h
B. 50 km/h
C. 70 km/h
e:
LESSON 6
This sign indicates, inside a built-up area, the end of a 30 km/h zone.
The car therefore continues within the built-up area.
The maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is:
- in the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region: 50 km/h
- in the Brussels-Capital Region: 30 km/h

q:
A driver has to make a long trip abroad.
How often should he ideally take a break?

A. Every half hour.
B. Every hour.
C. Every two hours.
e:
LESSON 13
During a long car journey, it is recommended to take a short break of at least 15 minutes about every two hours to prevent fatigue and stay alert. Make sure to get out of the car, stretch your legs, take deep breaths, and stay hydrated with water or light snacks.

q:
When must you take this sign into account?

A. Only on weekdays, 24 hours a day.
B. Only on weekdays, including Saturday, from 06:00 to 20:00.
C. Every day of the week, 24 hours a day.
e:
LESSON 30
These signs indicate the start and end of a low-emission zone (LEZ).
A low-emission zone is a defined area where certain vehicles are not allowed because they emit too many harmful substances.
A LEZ applies every day of the week, 24 hours a day, unless stated otherwise.
Each city or municipality can decide where a LEZ is introduced.

q:
Driving onto a level crossing when the red flashing light is operating and the barrier is descending is an offence of the …

A. … second degree.
B. … third degree.
C. … fourth degree.
e:
LESSON 4 / Offences
Fourth-degree offences are the most serious traffic violations.
They directly endanger people’s safety and, in the event of a collision, almost inevitably cause physical injury.
Examples include:
- ignoring the orders of an authorised person,
- not respecting the signalling at a level crossing,
- stopping or parking on a level crossing,
- encouraging excessive speed,
- organising speed races on public roads,
- overtaking on the left on a hill or in a bend when this is prohibited,
- reversing or driving in the opposite direction on a motorway or expressway.

q:
There is a traffic jam. The grey car drives on the left lane on this motorway. A motorcycle wants to overtake between the left and the middle lane. Where should the car driver drive on the lane?

A. As far to the left as possible.
B. In the middle.
C. As far to the right as possible.
e:
LESSON 4 – ‘The motorway’.
In a queue motorcycles are allowed to ride:
. between the two left lanes of traffic.
. at a maximum speed of 20 km/hour faster than the other vehicles.
. and at a maximum speed of 50 km/hour.
So the grey car drives as far as possible to the left, to give place.

q:
You are involved in an accident with a female cyclist. She remains lying on the road and does not move.
What do you do first?

A. Move the cyclist to the side of the road.
B. Park your car safely to clear the roadway.
C. Place a warning triangle.
e:
LESSON 29
The first thing to do is to prevent further accidents by placing a warning triangle.
An injured person must never be moved, unless there is immediate danger.
You call the emergency services (112).
In an accident involving injuries, the police must be notified.

B. 50 km/h.
C. 70 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
It is a roadway within a built-up area.
In the Walloon and Flemish Regions, the maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is 50 km/h.
In the Brussels Region, it is 30 km/h.
The yellow sign has nothing to do with speed — it is a priority-road sign indicating that you are driving on a priority road.

q:
Some cars are equipped with an automatic braking system.

A. I can fully trust this system 100%.
B. This allows me to be less focused while driving.
C. I must still remain attentive and drive anticipatively.
e:
LESSON 13
You cannot rely 100% on automatic braking systems. They may trigger unexpected braking or fail to activate when needed. These technologies are driving aids that reduce the risk of accidents, but they should never replace the driver’s vigilance. The driver must remain attentive and anticipate, since the technology still struggles with all road complexities.

q:
This girl is 8 years old. She is cycling on the roadway within a built-up area.
May she also ride on the raised pavement?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 3
If there is a cycle path, a cyclist must use it (unless it is not rideable).
It is forbidden to cycle on the pavement within a built-up area, except for children under 10, who may always ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.
Outside built-up areas, cyclists may, if there is no usable cycle path and provided they ride on the right-hand side of the road, ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.

At the top right, a sign warns drivers that the car’s lights must be on.
But which lights?

A. The dipped headlights.
B. The parking lights.
C. The fog lights.
e:
LESSON 11
When the dipped headlights (or the main beams) are on, the two parking lights at the front and the two red lights at the rear are also lit.
The dipped headlights must be on:
During the day:
in rain, when visibility is less than 200 meters;
in fog, when visibility is less than 200 meters;
in snowfall, when visibility is less than 200 meters;
or in other conditions (e.g. smoke from a fire) when visibility is less than 200 meters, such as in this tunnel.

q:

A. Sign 1.
B. Sign 2.
C. Sign 3.
e:
LESSON 4 – Explanation
- Sign 1 indicates the start of a motorway.
- Sign 2 indicates the start of a motor road.
- Sign 3 is placed when you enter a motorway via an on-ramp.
This sign means that you must give way to traffic already driving on the motorway.
If necessary, you must stop and wait on the on-ramp before merging.

q:
It is 30 November.
What is the maximum permitted speed when driving with studded tyres within a built-up area in the Brussels-Capital Region?

A. Driving with studded tyres is not allowed.
B. Maximum 60 km/h.
C. Maximum 30 km/h.
e:
LESSON 31
Studded tyres may be used between 1 November and 31 March on vehicles with a maximum authorised mass up to 3.5 tonnes.
Speed limits with studded tyres:
- Motorways and roads with 2×2 lanes separated by a central reservation: maximum 90 km/h.
- Ordinary roads: maximum 60 km/h.
- Within a built-up area: the maximum speed applicable there.
In the Brussels-Capital Region, this is 30 km/h.

- Motorways and roads with 2×2 lanes separated by a central reservation: maximum 90 km/h.
- Ordinary roads: maximum 60 km/h.
- Within a built-up area: the maximum speed applicable there.
In the Brussels-Capital Region, this is 30 km/h.

q:
What is the maximum permitted speed in a cycle zone in the Brussels-Capital Region?

A. Walking pace.
B. 20 km/h.
C. 30 km/h.
e:
LESSON 6
A cycle zone consists of one or more public roads where cyclists (and riders of bicycles and speed pedelecs) are the main road users, while motor vehicles are also allowed.
In a cycle zone, motor vehicles (including mopeds) may not overtake cyclists, speed pedelecs or bicycles up to 1 m wide.
The maximum permitted speed is 30 km/h.
A cycle zone ends at the “END OF ZONE” road sign.

q:
There is a traffic jam on a motorway with three lanes in the same direction.
Where should the vehicles on the middle lane position themselves?

A. As far left as possible.
B. In the middle of the lane.
C. As far right as possible.
e:
LESSON 4
In a traffic jam, an emergency corridor must be created between the leftmost lane and the adjacent lane.
Drivers on the left lane move as far left as possible.
Drivers on the middle and right lanes move as far right as possible to allow emergency vehicles to pass.

q:
This young man is leaving a bar and wants to drive away.
May the police officer require him to take a saliva test?

A. Yes, always.
B. Yes, but only if he shows clear signs of drug use.
C. No, because he is not driving.
e:
LESSON 28
The police may not randomly require someone to take a saliva test.
A saliva test may only be carried out if there is a suspicion of drug use, and this suspicion must be based on objective observations (such as behaviour, physical signs, smell, or driving behaviour).
Exception:
If you are involved in a traffic accident, a saliva test may always be taken.
If drugs are detected, you must hand in your driving licence for at least 12 hours.
An immediate withdrawal of the driving licence is possible.

LESSON 21
The road on which the white car is driving is not a one-way street.
- When turning left, the driver positions himself as close as possible to the centre line of the road.
- When turning right, he must position himself as far to the right as possible on the road.

q:
Which traffic sign do these shark teeth belong to?

A. To sign 1.
B. To sign 2.
C. To neither sign 1 nor sign 2.
e:
LESSON 19
An inverted triangle means that you must give way to drivers travelling on the road you intend to enter and that you must stop if necessary.
If there is no cross traffic, you do not have to stop and may enter the junction.
A line made up of inverted triangles, also called shark teeth, indicates the place where you must stop if necessary.
If stopping is not necessary, you do not need to come to a complete stop.

Do you have to switch on the left indicator if you want to pass it?

A. Yes.
B. No, because the road is wide enough here.

LESSON 16
Normally you drive on the right side of the roadway. If you want to pass that car, you make a lateral movement to the left. Every lateral movement must be signalled with the indicator.

q:
You have stopped at the stop line.
To whom must you give way?

A. To all road users.
B. To drivers on the crossing road.
e:
LESSON 19
A STOP means you must come to a complete stop at the stop line and yield to drivers who are on the public road you want to enter.
If there is no stop line on the road surface, you must stop at the sign.
If there are no drivers on the crossing road, you still have to make a full stop before entering the intersection.

q:
May the driver of the car overtake the cyclist on the left at this intersection?

A. No, because it is an intersection with right-of-way from the right.
B. Yes, if he leaves 1.5 meters of lateral space.
e:
LESSON 16
At an intersection where right-of-way from the right applies, a cyclist may be overtaken on the left.
When overtaking a cyclist (or moped) on the left, the driver must leave:
1 meter of lateral distance in built-up areas.
1.5 meters outside built-up areas.

q:
This boy is 1.45 metres tall.

A. He must sit in an adapted child seat.
B. He may sit in an adapted child seat.
C. He may no longer sit in an adapted child seat.
e:
LESSON 10
Children under 18 years old and smaller than 1.35 m must be transported in an approved child restraint system that meets the required standards.
A child taller than 1.35 m may still be transported in a special child seat.
Children under 18 may not be transported in the front seat in a rear-facing child seat, unless there is no front airbag or it can be deactivated.

q:
During an alcohol check, you test positive.
You can however prove that you did not drink alcohol, but that you are taking medication with the same effect as alcohol.
What is the consequence?

A. None, because you did not drink alcohol.
B. Punishable in the same way as if you had consumed alcohol.
e:
LESSON
If you take medication that has the same effects as alcohol (such as drowsiness or reduced reaction time), this is clearly stated in the leaflet.
In that case, you are not allowed to drive a vehicle.
Driving anyway is punishable in the same way as driving under the influence of alcohol.

q:
A driver has to make a long trip abroad.
How often should he ideally take a break?

A. Every half hour.
B. Every hour.
C. Every two hours.
e: During a long car journey, it is recommended to take a short break of at least 15 minutes about every two hours to prevent fatigue and stay alert. Make sure to get out of the car, stretch your legs, take deep breaths, and stay hydrated with water or light snacks.

q:
What should you do if your vehicle starts aquaplaning?

A. Brake hard and steer to regain control.
B. Release the accelerator, do not brake and keep the steering wheel straight.
C. Accelerate so that the tyres regain grip.
e:
LESSON 31
Aquaplaning is a phenomenon in which the tyres lose contact with the road because of a layer of water.
If your vehicle starts aquaplaning, release the accelerator, do not brake and keep the steering wheel as straight as possible.
Avoid sudden steering movements. Control will gradually return once the tyres regain contact with the road.

q:
The judge is obliged to impose a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle for speeding in a residential area by more than …

A. 20 km/h.
B. 30 km/h.
C. 40 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
If you exceed the maximum speed limit by more than 40 km/h on a motorway, a road for motor vehicles, or an ordinary road, you risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
In addition, if you exceed the maximum speed by more than 30 km/h in a built-up area, in a 30-zone, or in a residential area, you also risk a revocation of the right to drive a motor vehicle.
This is not a decision taken by the police, but by a judge in court, when your case is examined.

q:
In this bicycle zone, the driver of the car overtook an e-scooter riding at only 20 km/h.
Is this allowed?

A. Yes, because it is not a cyclist.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 3
A bicycle zone starts at the “begin bicycle zone” traffic sign and ends at the “end bicycle zone” traffic sign.
In a bicycle zone:
• cyclists, speed pedelecs and personal mobility devices (such as an e-scooter) may use the roadway;
• motor vehicles are allowed to drive there, but they are not allowed to overtake cyclists, e-scooters or similar vehicles;
• the maximum speed is 30 km/h.
➡️ Even if the e-scooter is riding at 20 km/h, overtaking is not allowed.

B. In the direction of a possible obstacle.

LESSON 31

If you keep looking at the danger, you are likely to steer towards it.
- Counter-steer.
- Press the clutch.
- Release the accelerator.

q:
You parked your car in a street and spent the night at your grandparents’ house.
The next morning you want to leave, but the street has become a school street.
Are you allowed to drive away?

A. Yes, but only at walking speed.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 6
To reduce traffic congestion at the start and end of the school day, a street near a school may be closed twice a day to motor vehicles. When this happens, the street becomes a school street. This is indicated by a C3 (no entry) traffic sign, together with an additional panel stating SCHOOL STREET.
Who may enter a school street?
– Pedestrians, cyclists, and speed pedelecs.
– Residents of the street or drivers who have a garage there (with a permit).
– Priority vehicles.
– Vehicles with a special permit.

clean_e:
LESSON 6

Who may enter a school street?
– Pedestrians, cyclists, and speed pedelecs.
– Residents of the street or drivers who have a garage there (with a permit).
– Priority vehicles.
– Vehicles with a special permit.

q:
This girl is 12 years old and 1.45 metres tall.
She is sitting in the back seat of the car and is wearing a seat belt.
Is that correct?

A. Yes, because she is 1.45 metres tall.
B. No, she must sit in a child seat because she is only 12 years old.
e:
LESSON 10
Children under 18 years old and smaller than 1.35 m must be transported in an approved child restraint system that meets the required standards.
A child taller than 1.35 m may still be transported in a special child seat.
Children under 18 may not be transported in the front seat in a rear-facing child seat, unless there is no front airbag or it can be deactivated.

q:

B. Yes, because the towing vehicle is a car.
e:
LESSON 10
This prohibition sign prohibits access to all vehicles (not only lorries) whose total length, including load and trailer, exceeds the length indicated on the sign.
This therefore also applies to drivers of a vehicle combination:
when your car is towing a caravan, you are driving a vehicle combination.
(Vehicle + trailer = vehicle combination)

q:
May the blue car cross the continuous white line to reach a petrol station?

A. Yes.
B. No, the driver must use another road.
e:
LESSON 22
A special traversable lane is not part of the carriageway.
You may not:
- drive on it,
- stop on it,
- park on it.
You are also not allowed to use it in the last metres before an intersection to position yourself for turning.
However:
You may cross a special traversable lane:
- to access your property, a car park or a petrol station;
- or to go around an obstacle.

q:
How far may an indivisible load extend beyond the rear of the vehicle if it is properly marked?

A. 3 metres.
B. 1.5 metres.
C. 1 metre.
e:
LESSON 10
A load may extend up to 1 metre beyond the rear of the vehicle.
If the load is indivisible and cannot be shortened or folded, it may extend up to 3 metres beyond the rear, provided that it is properly marked with the required warning panel.
This load is not properly marked.

q:
You are approaching an intersection where an officer is standing with one arm extended horizontally.
What instruction is the officer giving?

A. You must turn right.
B. You may continue driving, but not turn right.
C. You must stop.
e:
LESSON 17
When an officer extends one or both arms horizontally, road users approaching the officer’s chest or back must stop.
Road users approaching the officer’s fingertips or side may continue onto the intersection to go straight, turn left, or turn right.
An officer with both arms extended horizontally gives exactly the same instruction as with only one arm extended horizontally.

q:
May a firefighter regulate traffic and give orders at the place where the fire brigade is intervening?

A. No, only the police can do that.
B. Yes, a firefighter can give orders.
C. Yes, they can give orders and also draw up a report.
e:
LESSON 17
All members of the fire services present at the scene of an intervention have the authority to regulate road traffic.
Firefighters may give binding orders to road users but are not allowed to draw up an official report or place traffic signs. If a road user refuses to comply with the firefighter’s instructions, this must be reported afterwards to the police, who will issue the official report.

clean_e:
LESSON 17

q:
At what maximum speed may this car drive, inside a built-up area in the Flemish Region, past this sign?

A. 30 km/h
B. 50 km/h
C. 70 km/h
e:
LESSON 6
This sign indicates, inside a built-up area, the end of a 30 km/h zone.
The car therefore continues within the built-up area.
The maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is:
- in the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region: 50 km/h
- in the Brussels-Capital Region: 30 km/h

q:
An ordinary road divided into two lanes in Wallonia.
You are driving this car at 110 km/h. The police stop you.
What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. Your driving licence is immediately and temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to explain yourself in court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence if you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
If you drive in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area more than 20 km/h too fast, you also risk an immediate withdrawal.
This immediate withdrawal is done by the police, because it is a serious offence.
For any serious offence, your driving licence can be immediately withdrawn.

q:
You approach this bus stop by car and you see that the bus has its hazard warning lights on.
Do you have to slow down and give way?

A. Yes, only outside built-up areas.
B. Yes, only in built-up areas.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 22
You must give way when a bus wants to leave a bus stop in a built-up area.
In that case, the left indicator must be flashing, not the hazard warning lights.
When the hazard warning lights are on, this simply means that the bus is stopped to allow passengers to get on or off.

B. 130 cm.

Each passenger: 40 cm.
Total: 55 + 40 + 40 = 135 cm.

q:
You have to drive downhill for a long distance. What should you do?

A. Let the car roll downhill in neutral.
B. Shift to a lower gear.
C. Keep driving and brake regularly.
e:
LESSON 31
Even when driving downhill, you should engage a lower gear.
Rule of thumb: descend in the same gear you would use to climb the same hill.
This allows engine braking and prevents brake wear.

q:
Traffic is moving very slowly and keeps stopping.
If traffic comes to a halt again, are you allowed to stop on a pedestrian crossing?

A. Yes.
B. No, you must stop before the pedestrian crossing.
e:
LESSON 25
You are not allowed to stop or park on a pedestrian crossing.
This rule is strict.
⚠️ Even in a traffic jam, you may not stop, not even briefly, on a pedestrian crossing to follow traffic.

q:
If your vehicle breaks down on the hard shoulder of a motorway, do you also have to place a warning triangle?

A. Yes.
B. No, only if the vehicle is on the carriageway.
e:
LESSON 4 – Explanation
Even when your vehicle has broken down on the hard shoulder, placing a warning triangle is mandatory.
On a motorway, it must be placed 100 metres in front of the vehicle.
On other roads, the distance is 50 metres (or 30 metres in built-up areas).
In built-up areas, the warning triangle may also be placed on the roof of the vehicle.
The triangle must be visible from at least 50 metres for approaching drivers.

On a motorway, it must be placed 100 metres in front of the vehicle.
On other roads, the distance is 50 metres (or 30 metres in built-up areas).
In built-up areas, the warning triangle may also be placed on the roof of the vehicle.
The triangle must be visible from at least 50 metres for approaching drivers.

q:
When you carry out a manoeuvre, to whom must you give priority?

A. To drivers only.
B. To all road users.
e:
LESSON 21
A manoeuvre is an action you perform with your vehicle that is not specifically regulated by the traffic rules (for example, reversing).
When you carry out a manoeuvre, you must:
- observe very carefully first,
- give priority to all road users (both drivers and pedestrians).

q:
This driver wants to turn left at this junction.
Even if there are no other road users, the driver must first come to a complete stop before entering the junction.

A. True.
B. False.
e:
LESSON 19
The give-way sign (inverted triangle) means that you must yield to other drivers and, if necessary, come to a complete stop.
If there are no other drivers, you don’t need to stop completely and may enter the junction.
If there is a STOP sign, however, you must come to a complete stop.

q:
May the truck overtake a horse rider on the left?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 16
This prohibition sign indicates that trucks or combinations with a maximum authorised mass exceeding 3500 kg may not overtake:
- a combination of vehicles, or
- any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a truck on the left after this sign.
A bus may also overtake, since it is not a truck.

e:
LESSON 4
The zipper principle does not apply when entering a motorway.
So you do not have to drive to the end of the ramp before merging.
You may merge wherever it is safe to do so.

- overtake a combination of vehicles, or

- up to and including the next intersection, or
- it ends earlier if you see the end-of-prohibition sign.

But the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

q:
You approach this junction and you want to cross it.
The oncoming car wants to turn left.
Do you have to give it priority?
e:
LESSON 21 – ‘Priority and changing directions’.
If the traffic lights work, the board under the lights has no meaning.
The traffic light is green, so you're allowed to drive straight through.
Your oncoming vehicle (if it also has green light), wants to turn and must want to give priority.

q:
You are approaching this intersection. Where must you stop?

A. Just before the stop line.
B. 1 metre before the stop line.
C. Just before the STOP sign.
e:
LESSON 19
You must always come to a complete stop when there is a STOP sign, even if there are no other road users. You stop just before the stop line. If there is no stop line, you stop level with the STOP sign. You must give way to other drivers on the road you intend to enter.

q:
Is it true that this woman does not have to wear a seat belt while reversing?

A. Yes.
B. No, she must always wear a seat belt.
e:
LESSON 10
In general, a driver must wear a seat belt at all times.
However, the law provides a number of exceptions.
The following persons are exempt from wearing a seat belt:
- a driver who is reversing;
- taxi drivers while transporting passengers;
- postal workers delivering parcels from house to house
(this does not apply to everyone who delivers parcels;)
- authorised persons and emergency services, when required for their duties.

Is this car allowed to park at that spot in this residential area?

A. No, it must park on the shoulder.
B. No.
C. Yes, because in a residential area, you are allowed to park on the left or right side of the roadway.
e: In a residential area, cars may only park in places specifically designated for parking. They may be parked on either the left or right side of the roadway, depending on the direction of traffic.

q:
The white car is approaching an intersection and wants to turn left.
At the intersection, a police officer is standing with one arm stretched out horizontally.
What must the driver do?

A. The driver must stop.
B. The driver may go straight ahead or turn right.
e:
LESSON 17
When a police officer holds one or both arms stretched out horizontally, road users approaching the officer’s front or back must stop.
Road users approaching from the sides or towards the fingertips may enter the intersection and go straight, turn left or turn right.
A police officer with two arms stretched out horizontally gives exactly the same instruction as with one arm stretched out.

q:
Both cars are driving on this motorway at the maximum permitted speed.
What safe following distance must they keep?

A. 50 metres.
B. 55 metres.
C. 60 metres.
e:
LESSON 13 – Explanation:
The faster you drive, the greater the distance you must keep.
You can calculate this distance using a simple formula:
the speed divided by two.
In this example, the cars are driving at 120 km/h.
The safe following distance is therefore: 120 ÷ 2 = 60 metres.
At 100 km/h, it becomes 100 ÷ 2 = 50 metres.

q:
A roadway divided into lanes outside a built-up area.
May the red truck overtake the white car on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
This prohibition sign indicates that trucks or combinations with a maximum authorised mass exceeding 3500 kg may not overtake:
- a combination of vehicles, or
- any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a truck on the left after this sign.
A bus may also overtake, since it is not a truck.

- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a truck on the left after this sign.
A bus may also overtake, since it is not a truck.

q:
The ABS light is on. What do you do?

A. You have to stop immediately, because you have no brakes anymore.
B. You drive on, but you have it checked at the garage.
C. You pull the fuse, so that the light stops burning.
e: An ABS light on your car’s dashboard means there is an issue with the car’s anti-lock braking system. The Anti-lock Braking System &#40;ABS&#41; is a safety system that prevents the wheels of a vehicle from locking up when braking, and this warning tells you that the system is not working as intended. The ABS light is usually an amber, yellow, or orange light that spells out “ABS” and turns on during startup for a few seconds. If the ABS light stays on, then you might be dealing with a faulty ABS control module, low brake fluid levels, and a faulty wheel speed sensor.

The ABS light is on. What do you do? 
A. You have to stop immediately, because you have no brakes anymore. 
B. You drive on, but you have it checked at the garage. 
C. You pull the fuse, so that the light stops burning.
clean_e: An ABS light on your car’s dashboard means there is an issue with the car’s anti-lock braking system. The Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) is a safety system that prevents the wheels of a vehicle from locking up when braking, and this warning tells you that the system is not working as intended. The ABS light is usually an amber, yellow, or orange light that spells out “ABS” and turns on during startup for a few seconds. If the ABS light stays on, then you might be dealing with a faulty ABS control module, low brake fluid levels, and a faulty wheel speed sensor.

Where must the reflective safety vest be kept?

A. In the passenger compartment.
B. In the trunk.
C. In the passenger compartment or in the trunk.

LESSON 5
When a vehicle stops on a motorway or a road for motor vehicles and the driver gets out at a place where parking is prohibited, the driver must wear a reflective safety vest.

q:
You are involved in an accident.
May the police ask you to take a saliva test?

A. Only if there is a strong suspicion of drug use.
B. Yes, in the event of an accident, always.
e:
LESSON 28
Normally, a saliva test may only be carried out if the police suspect drug use.
BUT NOTE: if you are involved in an accident, the police may always require a saliva test.
If the result is above the permitted limit:
- your driving licence must be immediately surrendered for 12 hours;
- an immediate withdrawal of the driving licence is also possible;
- after the 12 hours, a new saliva test is carried out.
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving ban of up to 5 years.

q:
You have a breakdown on a motorway.
When must the driver wear the safety vest?

A. Always.
B. From nightfall until daylight.
e:
LESSON 4
The driver must wear a high-visibility vest as soon as he or she leaves the vehicle.
That is why the vest must be kept inside the car and within reach (not in the boot).
Passengers are not required to wear one, but it is strongly recommended.

Other vehicles (for example a moped) may be overtaken on the left in a bicycle street, provided that the maximum permitted speed is not exceeded.

q:
This driver holds a full category B driving licence.
The M.A.M. of the van is 2,700 kg.
The trailer may have a M.A.M. of …

A. 700 kg.
B. 750 kg.
C. 800 kg.
e:
LESSON 10
A vehicle with a M.A.M. of 3,500 kg may tow a trailer with a M.A.M. of up to 750 kg.
If the M.A.M. of the towing vehicle is less than 3,500 kg, the trailer’s M.A.M. may be higher, provided that the combined M.A.M. does not exceed 3,500 kg.
➡️ 2,700 kg + 800 kg = 3,500 kg → permitted.

q:
What is car sharing?

A. With several people at the same time use the car to reach a destination together.
B. Occasionally using a car at times fixed in advance via reservation system.
e:
LESSON 9 – ‘M.A.W. and M.G.W.’.
If you share a car, you use one car with several people at a different time. You use the car when you need it. When you don't need it, someone else can drive it. In this way, a car is used better and thoughtfully and not everyone has to buy a car.

q:
An intersection without traffic signs.
The driver of the white car stops briefly at the intersection and then wants to drive straight ahead. But the grey car suddenly turns left.

A. That is allowed, because the grey car comes from the right.
B. That is allowed, because the white car stopped.
C. That is not allowed, because the white car goes straight on.
e:
LESSON 21
Traffic going straight has priority over turning traffic.
Even if the driver of the white car stops briefly, he keeps his priority.

q:
How should you position your wheels when you are stopped at an intersection?

A. With the wheels turned in the direction you want to turn.
B. With the wheels pointing straight ahead.
e:
LESSON 21
When you stop at an intersection and intend to turn right, you must keep your wheels straight until you actually begin to turn. This prevents your vehicle from being pushed directly into the side street in case of an unexpected impact, which could create a dangerous situation or force you to give way to oncoming traffic. You only turn when it is safe to do so, taking into account other road users and pedestrians.

The four digits following the word DOT on the sidewall of a tire indicate the tire’s production date.

A. The first two digits show the week, and the last two the year.
B. The first two digits show the year, and the last two the week.
C. The four digits have nothing to do with the production date.

q:
A blind person is crossing the road at a place where there is no pedestrian crossing.
You stop your car — but are you required to do so?

A. No, there is no pedestrian crossing, so you have priority.
B. You may stop; it is courteous behaviour.
C. You must give way to the blind person.
e:
LESSON 7
In Belgium, every driver (car, bicycle, etc.) is legally required to give way to a blind or visually impaired pedestrian,
recognisable by a white cane, when that person is crossing or clearly intends to cross.
This rule applies even if there is no pedestrian crossing.
It also applies to other persons with reduced mobility, such as those using a walking frame or rollator.

B. In the middle.
C. As far left as possible.

Vehicles on the left lane move as far to the left as possible, while vehicles on the adjacent lane move as far to the right to allow emergency vehicles to pass if necessary.

q:
There is fog and visibility is only 90 meters.
The white car is parked.
Which lights must be switched on?

A. The parking lights at the front and rear.
B. The parking lights and the dipped-beam headlights.
C. The parking lights, dipped-beam headlights and the rear fog light.
e:
LESSON 11
When you are stationary or parked on the road or on the verge: between sunset and sunrise; and whenever it is no longer possible to see clearly for about 200 meters, the following lights must be on:
at the front: one or two white or yellow parking lights
at the rear: one or two red lights.
In foggy weather, snowfall, or heavy rain, the dipped headlights or front fog lights and also the rear fog lights may be used, but it is not required.

q:
Does the priority to the right also apply in a supermarket car park?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 20
The priority to the right also generally applies in a supermarket car park, as it is usually considered a place accessible to public traffic.
Therefore, the normal road traffic rules apply.
Important
Leaving a parking space (forwards or backwards) is a special manoeuvre: the driver must give way to all other traffic.
If there are road signs, yield markings, stop signs or arrows on the ground, these priority rules must be followed.

If there are road signs, yield markings, stop signs or arrows on the ground, these priority rules must be followed.

q:
It’s snowing. When must you switch on your dipped-beam headlights?

A. Always when it’s snowing.
B. As soon as visibility is less than 100 metres.
C. As soon as visibility is less than 200 metres.
e:
LESSON 11
During the day, you must switch on your dipped-beam headlights:
- in rain, when you cannot see farther than 200 metres;
- in fog, when you cannot see farther than 200 metres;
- in snowfall, when you cannot see farther than 200 metres;
- or in other situations (e.g. smoke from a fire) when visibility is less than 200 metres.

q:
Where must the reflective safety vest be located?

A. Inside the interior of the car.
B. In the trunk of the car.
e:
LESSON 4 - 'Motorway'
The reflective safety vest, also known as the yellow vest, must be located inside the vehicle’s interior, within easy reach, and not in the trunk. It is recommended to store it in the glove compartment, under the driver’s seat, or in a door pocket so that it is immediately accessible to the driver if they need to exit the vehicle on a motorway or a road for motor vehicles.

q:
You want to leave the motorway.
Where should you brake?

A. Maximum 150 meters before the exit.
B. Maximum 50 meters before the exit.
C. Only once you are on the exit lane.
e:
LESSON 4
Of course, there is no rule that obliges you to still drive 120 km/h just before the exit. You may already drive a bit slower before reaching the exit. But actual braking, meaning pressing the brake pedal, should only be done once you are on the exit lane.

Is the car parked correctly?

A. Yes, because the car is in front of the traffic sign.
B. No.

LESSON 26
A broken white line divides the roadway into lanes.
This means that parking on the roadway is not allowed. You may stop only briefly to let someone get in or out.
Waiting for someone counts as parking.

e:

- drive,
- stop,
- park.

You may also cross a bus lane:
- at a junction,
- to access a property (garage, shop, petrol station, house …).

q:
A car may not park on the roadway within 5 metres before a pedestrian or cyclist crossing.
But may a driver stop there to let someone get in?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 7
It is forbidden to park or stop on a pedestrian crossing, even to let someone get in or out.
It is also forbidden to park or stop on the roadway within 5 metres before the crossing (parking on the verge is allowed if possible).
The same rule applies to crossings for cyclists and two-wheeled mopeds.
After the crossing, parking and stopping are allowed, unless prohibited by traffic signs.

q:
At what maximum speed may this car drive past the yellow sign within a built-up area in the Walloon Region?

A. 30 km/h.
B. 50 km/h.
C. 70 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
It is a roadway within a built-up area.
In the Walloon and Flemish Regions, the maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is 50 km/h.
In the Brussels Region, it is 30 km/h.
The yellow sign has nothing to do with speed — it is a priority-road sign indicating that you are driving on a priority road.
