q:
It is night, outside a built-up area.
While the blue car is overtaking, the distracted driver of the white car suddenly swerves to the left.
What may the driver of the blue car do?

A. He may only use the main beams because it is night.
B. He may use the horn to avoid an accident.
e:
LESSON 11
When may you use the horn?
During the day:
Inside and outside built-up areas: to warn others and prevent an accident.
Outside built-up areas: to warn a driver you intend to overtake.
Between dusk and dawn:
Only in case of imminent danger.
In all other cases, you must flash your headlights alternately instead of using the horn.

A. As short as possible.
B. As wide as possible.

LESSON 24

- When turning left, you take the turn as wide as possible.

q:
An accident without injuries.
What should you do if one of the drivers refuses to sign the European accident report?

A. You sign it and inform the insurance company.
B. You call the police.
e:
LESSON 29
In an accident without injuries, you normally do not need to call the police, but the European accident report must be completed and signed by all drivers involved.
By signing, you confirm that you agree with the information written on the form.
Once everyone has signed, nothing can be changed.
If one of the drivers refuses to sign the report, you call the police.

q:
What is the maximum speed the white car may drive in Wallonia on this road?

A. 50 km/h.
B. 70 km/h.
C. 90 km/h.
e:
LESSON 6
The traffic sign is a place-name sign.
It does not indicate the start of a built-up area.
On a regular road in Wallonia, the maximum speed limit is 90 km/h.
For comparison:
- in Flanders, the limit is 70 km/h,
- in the Brussels-Capital Region, it is also 70 km/h.

q: May the car overtake the truck on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
This prohibition sign indicates that trucks or combinations with a maximum authorised mass exceeding 3500 kg may not overtake:
- a combination of vehicles, or
- any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a truck on the left after this sign.
A bus may also overtake, since it is not a truck.

q:
Traffic is moving very slowly.
Is this lady allowed to quickly call her employer, to say that she will arrive a bit later?
e:
LESSON 31
Electronic devices such as a phone, tablet (…) distract the driver.
That is why drivers are not allowed to use, hold or manipulate a mobile electronic device with a screen while driving, unless the device is placed in a proper holder attached to the vehicle.
Electronic devices may be used when the car is standing still or parked. But stopping at a traffic light or in a traffic jam is not considered as ‘standing still’.

q:
A roadway divided into lanes inside a built-up area.
May the white car overtake the blue car on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
The red circular prohibition sign indicates that it is forbidden for drivers to:
- overtake a combination of vehicles, or
- overtake any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle,
but the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

B. 70 km/h.

- on ordinary roads in all regions: 60 km/h.

q:
The police can ask any driver to take a breath test.
Is that also the case for a saliva test?

A. Yes, a saliva test can be imposed on any driver.
B. Yes, if there is, based on objective findings, a suspicion of drug use.
e:
LESSON 28
In Belgium, there is zero tolerance for drugs in traffic. The police regularly carry out drug checks to determine whether you have certain prohibited substances in your body.
But a saliva test may only be carried out if the police suspect that you are under the influence of drugs, and this suspicion must be based on objective findings.

q:
It is 4:05 pm when the car stops.
The driver asks the passenger to set the parking disc.
Where must the arrow be set?

A. At 4:00 pm.
B. At 4:15 pm.
C. At 4:30 pm.
e:
LESSON 27
On the parking disc, you set the arrow to the first marking after the time of arrival.
You may then park for two hours, counted from the time shown by the arrow,
unless a road sign indicates a different parking duration.
Afterwards, you must move the car; you are not allowed to change the time on the disc.
➡️ The first marking after 4:05 pm is 4:30 pm.

q:
Your car is parked in this street, which suddenly becomes a school street for half an hour.
Are you allowed to leave the street with your car?

A. No, you must wait for half an hour.
B. Yes, if you drive at walking speed.
e:
LESSON 6
To reduce traffic at the start and end of the school day, a street near a school can be temporarily closed to motor vehicles. It then becomes a school street.
The closure is indicated by sign C3 (no entry) together with an additional sign stating SCHOOL STREET.
In principle, a permit is required to drive in a school street. However, leaving a parking space is allowed, provided that you drive at walking speed and proceed with extra caution.

q:
An adult wants to cross the road with young children.
How should you react?

A. You continue driving because there is no pedestrian crossing.
B. You stop and let the children cross calmly.
C. You pay extra attention and are ready to brake if a child suddenly steps onto the road.
e:
DEFENSIVE DRIVING
Children can behave unpredictably and may suddenly step onto the road.
You adjust your speed, keep the situation under control, and are ready to brake immediately.

- when passing in opposite directions (meeting) a cyclist or moped rider.

q:
The car is stationary while three children are getting out.
Is this driver allowed to hold her mobile phone in her hand?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON
Yes. When a vehicle is stationary to allow passengers to get in or out, the driver may hold a mobile phone in their hand.
This is considered “being stationary” under the road code.
Note: stopping at a red light, a level crossing or in a traffic jam is not considered “being stationary” in this sense. In those cases, holding a phone is not allowed.

q:
May the car stop at that place within a built-up area to let a passenger get in?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 7
It is forbidden to park or stop on a pedestrian crossing, even to let someone get in or out.
It is also forbidden to park or to stop on the roadway within 5 metres before the crossing (parking on the verge is allowed if possible).
The same rule applies to crossings for cyclists and two-wheeled mopeds.
After the crossing, parking and stopping are allowed, unless prohibited by traffic signs.

q:
What happens if you refuse to take a saliva test?

A. You receive a temporary driving ban of 6 hours.
B. You receive a temporary driving ban of 10 hours.
C. You receive a temporary driving ban of 12 hours.
e:
LESSON 28
A saliva test may only be administered when the police suspect drug use.
Although Belgian law allows you to refuse the test, it is strongly advised to cooperate. If you refuse, the police must call a doctor to evaluate your reasons.
If the doctor concludes that you have no legal grounds for refusing, you must pay the doctor’s costs and you will receive a temporary 12-hour driving ban.
During a saliva test, a swab is used to collect saliva from your mouth to determine the presence of drugs.
If the result exceeds the permitted value:
– you must immediately surrender your licence for 12 hours
– an immediate withdrawal of the licence is possible
– after 12 hours, a new saliva test is required
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving-ban sentence of up to 5 years.

If the doctor concludes that you have no legal grounds for refusing, you must pay the doctor’s costs and you will receive a temporary 12-hour driving ban.
During a saliva test, a swab is used to collect saliva from your mouth to determine the presence of drugs.
If the result exceeds the permitted value:
– you must immediately surrender your licence for 12 hours
– an immediate withdrawal of the licence is possible
– after 12 hours, a new saliva test is required
Offenders also risk heavy fines and a driving-ban sentence of up to 5 years.

q:
The white car wants to reverse into a parking space.
What is correct?

A. The driver must give way to all road users.
B. The driver must give way to all drivers.
C. Approaching drivers must stop and give way to the white car.
e:
LESSON 21
Manoeuvres include:
• changing lanes;
• crossing the carriageway;
• entering a parking space;
• leaving a parking space;
• leaving a property;
• turning around in a street;
• reversing.
When carrying out a manoeuvre, you must:
• do so with great care;
• not endanger any other road users (including pedestrians);
• give way to all road users.

q:
The carriageway is narrow.
May the driver of the car partly drive onto the verge in order to cross or pass the cyclist safely?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 15
When overtaking or crossing / passing a cyclist outside built-up areas, a minimum lateral distance of 1,5 metres must be respected.
If the carriageway is not wide enough, the driver may partly drive onto the verge to keep this distance.
Within built-up areas, the required lateral distance is 1 metres.

q:
The blue car is entering the intersection.
Is the driver allowed to do that?

A. Yes, because he is going straight ahead.
B. Yes, because he has priority from the right.
e:
LESSON 20
At intersections where: there is no authorized officer, no traffic lights, and no signs regulating priority, the rule of priority to the right applies. This means that every driver (of a car, motorcycle, two-wheeled moped, or bicycle) must yield to a driver coming from the right, except when driving on a roundabout or when the driver coming from the right is coming from a prohibited direction.

q:
You continuously drive in a low gear at high engine speed.
What is the consequence?

A. Lower fuel consumption.
B. Higher fuel consumption.
C. No impact on the environment.
e:
LESSON 30
The higher the engine speed, the more fuel the engine consumes. This leads to higher emissions and is harmful to the environment.
Environmentally friendly driving means shifting up early and keeping the engine speed as low as possible without straining the engine.

q:
You are driving at 47 km/h in a residential area in Wallonia. What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence.
C. You will have to appear before the court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone, or a residential area (woonerf), you already risk an immediate withdrawal if you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a disqualification from driving as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not decided by the police but by the judge when your case is handled in court.

You are driving at 47 km/h in a residential area in Wallonia. What are the consequences?
A. You receive a fine.
B. You receive an immediate temporary withdrawal of your driving licence. 
C. You will have to appear before the court.
clean_e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence as soon as you drive more than 30 km/h too fast on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
However, in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone, or a residential area (woonerf), you already risk an immediate withdrawal if you drive more than 20 km/h too fast.
You risk a disqualification from driving as soon as you exceed the speed limit by more than 40 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
In a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area, you already receive a disqualification once you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h.
This is not decided by the police but by the judge when your case is handled in court.

q:
After a breath test, your result is 0.25 mg/l. What happens?

A. You may leave because this is below the limit.
B. You receive a 3-hour driving ban.
C. You receive a 6-hour driving ban.
e:
LESSON 28
In a breath analysis, you blow into a device that measures the alcohol concentration in the alveolar air you exhale. The exact result appears on the screen (e.g. 0.25 mg/l).
- Less than 0.22 mg/l: you may continue to drive.
- 0.22 to less than 0.35 mg/l: you must surrender your licence for 3 hours. This is an offence and you will receive a fine.
- 0.35 mg/l or more: you must surrender your licence for 6 hours. An immediate withdrawal is possible. After those 6 hours, a new breath test is required.

After a breath test, your result is 0.25 mg/l. What happens?

clean_e:
LESSON 28
In a breath analysis, you blow into a device that measures the alcohol concentration in the alveolar air you exhale. The exact result appears on the screen (e.g. 0.25 mg/l).
- Less than 0.22 mg/l: you may continue to drive.
- 0.22 to less than 0.35 mg/l: you must surrender your licence for 3 hours. This is an offence and you will receive a fine.
- 0.35 mg/l or more: you must surrender your licence for 6 hours. An immediate withdrawal is possible. After those 6 hours, a new breath test is required.

This sign marks the beginning of a bicycle zone.

The maximum permitted speed in a bicycle zone is 30 km/h.

q:
An adult wants to cross the road with young children.
How should you react?

A. You continue driving because there is no pedestrian crossing.
B. You stop and let the children cross calmly.
C. You pay extra attention and are ready to brake if a child suddenly steps onto the road.

DEFENSIVE DRIVING
Children can behave unpredictably and may suddenly step onto the road.
You adjust your speed, keep the situation under control, and are ready to brake immediately.

q:
It is nighttime. The car in front of you is driving slowly.
Are you allowed to use the horn to signal that you are going to overtake that car?

A. Yes, but only outside built-up areas.
B. Yes.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 11
When are you allowed to use the horn?
During the day:
Inside and outside built-up areas: to warn others and prevent an accident.
Outside built-up areas: to warn a driver you want to overtake.
Between sunset and sunrise:
Only in case of imminent danger.
In other situations, instead of the horn, you must use the lights: flashing alternately with high and low beam.

q:
You are driving this car at 90 km/h. The police stop you.
What are the consequences?

A. You receive a fine.
B. Your driving licence is immediately and temporarily withdrawn.
C. You will have to explain yourself in court.
e:
LESSON 12
You risk an immediate withdrawal of your driving licence if you exceed the speed limit by more than 30 km/h on motorways, expressways or ordinary roads.
If you drive in a built-up area, a 30 km/h zone or a residential area more than 20 km/h too fast, you also risk an immediate withdrawal.
This immediate withdrawal is done by the police, because it is a serious offence.
For any serious offence, your driving licence can be immediately withdrawn.

q:
May the car overtake the horse-drawn cart on the left?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 16
The red circular prohibition sign indicates that it is forbidden for drivers to:
- overtake a combination of vehicles, or
- overtake any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- ends earlier if you see the “end of overtaking prohibition” sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle,
but the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

q:
You are driving in the inner lane of a two-lane roundabout. What must you do to exit?

A. Stop and wait until the cars in the outer lane have passed.
B. Exit directly, you have priority over the cars in the outer lane.
C. Keep circulating until the outer lane is free so you can exit.
e:
LESSON 19
To exit a two-lane roundabout from the inner lane, you must first change lanes to the outer lane, using your right indicator. You must give way to vehicles already driving in the outer lane. If you cannot do this in time, it is safer to make another loop around the roundabout rather than blocking traffic or creating a dangerous situation.

q:
To check the oil level, the car must be on a flat surface.
But when should you check the oil level?

A. When the engine is cold.
B. After driving a few kilometres so the oil is warm.
e:
LESSON 31
Engine oil is one of the most important fluids in your vehicle. It lubricates moving parts, prevents overheating and wear, and helps keep the engine clean.
To get a reliable reading, you should check the oil level when the engine is cold and the car is parked on a level surface. If the oil is still warm, it may not have fully drained back into the oil pan, leading to an inaccurate reading.

q: You have been drinking alcohol and you fear a police check. Is there any medicine that can quickly lower your blood alcohol level?
e:
LESSON 28
There is no medicine or other remedy (such as drinking coffee) that can make your blood alcohol level drop faster. Only time allows your body to break down alcohol. On the contrary, some medicines even intensify the effects of alcohol.

q:
When are you allowed to drive with snow chains on your tires?

A. From November 1st until March 31st.
B. When there is snow or ice on the road.
e:
LESSON 31
In Belgium, snow chains may only be used when there is snow or ice on the road. Vehicles equipped with chains are not subject to any special speed limit, but drivers must always adapt their speed to the road conditions.
For safety reasons, it is preferable to place snow chains on all four wheels. If only two are used, they must be fitted on the driving wheels.

B. You slow down.

LESSON 1

q:
This cyclist is 16 years old. There is no cycle path.
May she ride there outside a built-up area?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 3
If there is a cycle path, a cyclist must use it (unless it is not rideable).
It is forbidden to ride a bicycle on the pavement within a built-up area, except for children under 10, who may always ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.
Outside built-up areas, cyclists may, if there is no usable cycle path and provided they ride on the right-hand side of the road, ride on the pavement or on the raised verge.

q: May a user of a motorized personal mobility device be overtaken by motor vehicles in a bicycle zone?
e:
LESSON 3
Users of motorized personal mobility devices are treated the same as cyclists.
In a bicycle zone, cyclists may not be overtaken by motor vehicles. This overtaking ban also applies to motorcycles and mopeds of class A and B. The speed pedelec, however, is considered a bicycle and may overtake other cyclists.
On the other hand, cyclists may overtake each other and other road users. Overtaking must always be done on the left. A cyclist may also pass stationary vehicles on the right.
Priority vehicles (police, fire brigade, ambulance with siren and flashing light) are still allowed to overtake cyclists. If a priority vehicle is approaching, clear the roadway.
clean_q: May a user of a motorized personal mobility device be overtaken by motor vehicles in a bicycle zone?
clean_e:
LESSON 3
Users of motorized personal mobility devices are treated the same as cyclists.
In a bicycle zone, cyclists may not be overtaken by motor vehicles. This overtaking ban also applies to motorcycles and mopeds of class A and B. The speed pedelec, however, is considered a bicycle and may overtake other cyclists.
On the other hand, cyclists may overtake each other and other road users. Overtaking must always be done on the left. A cyclist may also pass stationary vehicles on the right.
Priority vehicles (police, fire brigade, ambulance with siren and flashing light) are still allowed to overtake cyclists. If a priority vehicle is approaching, clear the roadway.

q:
It is dusk inside a built-up area.
Before the driver of the white car started overtaking, he briefly flashed the high beams.
Was he allowed to do that?

A. Yes.
B. No, because he is driving inside a built-up area.
e:
LESSON 11
When may you use the horn?
During the day:
Inside and outside built-up areas: to warn others and prevent an accident.
Outside built-up areas: to warn a driver you intend to overtake.
Between dusk and dawn:
Only in case of imminent danger.
In all other cases, instead of using the horn, you should flash the headlights (switch between high and low beams).

q:
After which signs do you have priority?

A. Only after sign 1.
B. Only after signs 1 and 2.
C. After all three signs.
e:
LESSON 19
- Sign 1 – B21: Narrow passage.
You have priority over traffic coming from the opposite direction.
- Sign 2 – A39: Two-way traffic after a one-way section.
This sign does not grant priority.
- Sign 3 – B19: Narrow passage.
You must give priority to oncoming traffic — so you do not have priority.

q:
From what alcohol level in the breath are you committing an offence?

A. From 0.22 mg/l.
B. From 0.25 mg/l.
C. From 0.35 mg/l.
e:
LESSON 28
- If you have less than 0.22 mg/l, you may continue driving.
- If the result is 0.22 mg/l or more, but below 0.35 mg/l, you must hand over your driving licence for 3 hours.
It is an offence and you will receive a fine.
- If you have 0.35 mg/l or more, you must hand over your driving licence for 6 hours.
An immediate withdrawal of your licence is also possible, and you will receive a fine.
After those 6 hours, a new breath test or breath analysis will follow.

q:
Are you allowed to overtake the motorcycle on the left at this intersection?

A. Yes.
B. Yes, if you leave sufficient lateral distance.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 16
At intersections where no traffic signs regulate priority, or where this road sign is present and therefore the priority to the right applies, it is forbidden to overtake on the left:
a vehicle with a trailer,
a vehicle with more than two wheels,
or a motor vehicle with two wheels.
A cyclist is not a motor vehicle, so you are allowed to overtake them. You may also overtake a personal mobility device.

q:
After which traffic sign can you commit a 4th-degree offence?

A. Sign 1.
B. Sign 2.
C. Sign 3.
e:
4th-degree offence
Fourth-degree offences are the most serious traffic violations.
They directly endanger the safety of people and, in a collision, almost inevitably lead to physical injury.
Examples include:
• ignoring the orders of an authorised person,
• not respecting the signalling at a level crossing,
• stopping or parking a vehicle on a level crossing,
• encouraging excessive speed,
• organising or taking part in speed races on public roads,
• overtaking on the left on a hill or in a bend when prohibited,
• reversing or driving in the opposite direction on a motorway or an expressway.

q:
It is freezing.
Are you allowed to briefly press the accelerator from time to time to keep the engine warm?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 30
No. You are not allowed to press the accelerator to keep the engine warm. This causes unnecessary noise and air pollution.
It is also recommended to switch off the engine if you have to stop for more than 30 seconds (for example at a level crossing or a bridge).
Unnecessary idling or revving the engine while stationary is therefore not allowed.

q:
You are approaching an intersection where an officer is standing with one arm extended horizontally.
What instruction is the officer giving?

A. You must turn left.
B. You may continue driving, but not turn left.
C. You must stop.
e:
LESSON 17
When an officer extends one or both arms horizontally, road users approaching the officer’s chest or back must stop.
Road users approaching the officer’s fingertips or side may continue onto the intersection to go straight, turn left, or turn right.
An officer with both arms extended horizontally gives exactly the same instruction as with only one arm extended horizontally.

q:
The driver of this car saw no other road users and carefully entered the intersection without stopping.
Was he allowed to do that?

A. Yes, because there is no stop line on the road.
B. Yes, because he is turning right.
C. No, he must stop first.
e:
LESSON 19
A STOP means you must come to a complete stop at the stop line and yield to drivers on the public road you want to enter.
If there is no stop line on the pavement, you must stop at the traffic sign.
Even if there are no drivers on the crossing road, you must still come to a complete stop before entering the intersection.

q:
Which traffic sign will you see when you want to enter the motorway via the acceleration lane?

A. Sign 1.
B. Sign 2.
C. Sign 3.
e:
LESSON 19
When entering the motorway, you must give way (and stop if necessary) to drivers already driving on the motorway.
You do not have to drive until the very end of the acceleration lane. You may merge as soon as it is safe.
When joining the motorway, no zip-merge is applied.

q:
May a driver hold his mobile phone to read a message while passengers are getting into the car?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON
You may use the device without a holder only if your vehicle is stationary or correctly parked.
The term “stationary” must be interpreted as defined in the Belgian Road Code:
“remaining stationary only for the time strictly necessary to allow passengers to get in or out of the vehicle, or to load or unload the vehicle.”
Briefly stopping at a traffic light, for example, is not considered ‘being stationary’ within the meaning of the Road Code.

q:
Can someone with epileptic seizures drive a car?

A. Yes.
B. Never, because they can always have another seizure.
C. Only after a seizure-free period can a doctor grant permission to drive.
e:
Explanation:
A person with epileptic seizures is generally not allowed to drive.
After a first seizure, there is a driving ban of 6 months.
Someone with chronic epilepsy who, after treatment, has been seizure-free for 1 year may, with approval from a specialist doctor, be allowed to drive again.
After a new seizure, there is again a driving ban of 1 year.

C. Only after a seizure-free period can a doctor grant permission to drive.
clean_e:
Explanation:
A person with epileptic seizures is generally not allowed to drive.
After a first seizure, there is a driving ban of 6 months.
Someone with chronic epilepsy who, after treatment, has been seizure-free for 1 year may, with approval from a specialist doctor, be allowed to drive again.
After a new seizure, there is again a driving ban of 1 year.

q:
You find someone unconscious in a car.
You have switched off the engine, turned on the hazard warning lights and placed the warning triangle.
What should you do next?

A. Carefully remove the person from the vehicle.
B. Check the breathing and start resuscitation if necessary.
C. Call 112.
e:
LESSON 29
- First ensure your own safety and that of others.
- Check the person and call 112 immediately if there is no response.
- Check breathing:
.. Look to see if the chest rises and falls.
.. Listen at the mouth and nose for breathing sounds.
- Is the person breathing?
.. Keep the airway open (tilt the chin upwards).
.. Continue to monitor breathing until emergency services arrive.
- Is the person not breathing or not breathing normally?
..Start resuscitation immediately (chest compressions and, if possible, rescue breaths).

On such a carriageway, parking is not allowed.
➡️ Stopping (for example to allow a passenger to get in or out) is allowed.

q: May the motorcycle overtake the truck on the left?
e:
LESSON 16
The red circular prohibition sign indicates that drivers are forbidden to:
- overtake a combination of vehicles, or
- overtake any vehicle with more than two wheels.
The no-overtaking rule applies:
- up to and including the next intersection, or
- it ends earlier if you see the end-of-prohibition sign.
A car driver may therefore overtake a two-wheeled motorcycle.
But the motorcyclist may not overtake a car, because a car has more than two wheels.

B. 70 km/h.
C. 90 km/h.

In the Walloon Region, it is 90 km/h.

q:
A tyre has four DOT digits (xxxx).
These digits indicate when the tyre was manufactured. What do they mean?

A. The first two digits indicate the year and the last two the week.
B. The first two digits indicate the week and the last two the year.
e:
LESSON 31
The DOT number (Department of Transportation) on a tyre indicates the manufacturing date.
It consists of 4 digits:
- the first two digits indicate the week of production;
- the last two digits indicate the year of production.

q:
The driver of a car overtakes a cyclist riding on the roadway on the left side.
How much lateral clearance must he keep here?

A. 1 metre.
B. 1.5 metres.
C. Sufficient distance.
e:
LESSON 15
This image clearly shows a situation outside a built-up area.
- On the roadway:
When overtaking a pedestrian walking on the roadway, or a cyclist or moped rider using the roadway, you must keep at least 1.5 metres of lateral clearance.
- On a cycle path:
If the cyclist or moped rider is on a cycle path, you must also keep this 1.5-metre distance. (This distance also applies when meeting a cyclist coming from the opposite direction.)
Inside built-up areas, the required lateral clearance is 1 metre.

q:
You are driving a diesel car. At what engine speed is it best to change gears?

A. 1000–1500.
B. 1500–2000.
C. 2000–2500.
e:
LESSON 30
LESSON 30
The correct rev range to shift gears with a diesel engine is 1500–2000 rpm.
Sequence of actions when shifting gears:
1. Fully press the clutch and simultaneously release the accelerator.
2. Shift into the correct gear.
3. Release the clutch until the biting point and gently press the accelerator.
4. Release the clutch completely and place your left foot beside the pedal.

q:
Zipper merging is required. Where should you merge?

A. As soon as possible.
B. As soon as it is safe.
C. Just before the lane ends.
e:
LESSON 2
Zipper merging is mandatory when a lane disappears and traffic is heavily slowed down or stopped (like in a traffic jam). You must merge just before the disappearing lane ends.
When entering a motorway, zipper merging is not used—instead, you must give priority to drivers already on the motorway.

What do you do?

A. You stop.
B. You stop and drive backwards.

Vehicles already in the intersection must leave it as quickly as possible, continuing in the direction they intended to go.

q:
You are driving your car to Switzerland.
What should you do with the tyre pressure before leaving?

A. Slightly reduce the pressure.
B. Slightly increase the pressure.
C. Do nothing.
e:
LESSON 31
For a long journey or when carrying a heavy load, it is recommended to slightly increase the tyre pressure by about 0.2 bar.
This improves stability, reduces overheating, and limits tyre wear.

q:
What is aquaplaning?

A. Very heavy rain causing the driver to lose visibility.
B. Tyres losing contact with the road surface due to a large amount of water.
C. Splashing water that the windscreen wipers cannot clear.
e:
LESSON 31
Aquaplaning is a phenomenon in which the tyres lose contact with the road because of a layer of water.
Instead of gripping the road surface, the vehicle slides on the water.
As a result, you can no longer steer, brake or accelerate normally.
It can be compared to water skiing.

q:
How tall must a child be to no longer be required to sit in an appropriate safety seat?

A. 1.35 metres.
B. 1.40 metres.
C. 1.45 metres.
e:
LESSON 10
Children under the age of 18 and smaller than 1m35 must be transported in an appropriate safety seat or child restraint system that complies with the prescribed standards. (A child taller than 1m35 may still be transported in a special child seat). Children under 18 years old may not be transported in the front seat in a rear-facing child seat, unless there is no front airbag or the airbag can be deactivated.

q:
It’s warm and you’re driving with the window open.
Does this affect fuel consumption?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 30
Driving with the window open affects fuel consumption.
When the window is open, the vehicle’s air resistance increases.
At low speeds this effect is limited, but at higher speeds the added drag causes a noticeable rise in fuel consumption — up to 5–10% more at highway speed.

q:
A colleague who lives in this street is ill.
Are you allowed to drive there by car to visit him?

A. Yes, but at walking pace.
B. Yes, but at walking pace and after 6 p.m.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 7
It is forbidden to drive a car in a Belgian pedestrian zone (sign F103).
Access is only allowed with a special permit, usually for residents or for loading and unloading during specific hours.
If you enter without permission, you risk a fine.
Drivers who are allowed to enter must:
• drive at walking pace
• always give way to pedestrians
• stop if necessary
• never endanger or hinder pedestrians

q: Are you allowed to overtake a moped on the left with a car in a bicycle zone?
e:
In a bicycle zone, motor vehicles are not allowed to overtake a cyclist, scooter, or any other means of transport considered equivalent to a bicycle.
Other vehicles (for example, a moped) may be overtaken on the left in a bicycle zone, provided that the maximum permitted speed is not exceeded.

q:
A car may not park on the roadway within 5 metres before a pedestrian or cyclist crossing.
But may a driver stop there to let someone get in?

A. Yes.
B. No.
e:
LESSON 7
It is forbidden to park or stop on a pedestrian crossing, even to let someone get in or out.
It is also forbidden to park or stop on the roadway within 5 metres before the crossing (parking on the verge is allowed if possible).
The same rule applies to crossings for cyclists and two-wheeled mopeds.
After the crossing, parking and stopping are allowed, unless prohibited by traffic signs.

Overtaking on the left at intersections where the priority-to-the-right rule applies is prohibited, except in this specific case.

q:
From how many cyclists in a group are two road captains required?

A. From 15 participants.
B. From 26 participants.
C. From 51 participants.
e:
LESSON 3
Cyclists riding in a group of 51 to 150 participants must be accompanied by two road captains.
They are not required to use the cycle paths and may ride two abreast on the roadway, provided the group stays together.

q:
The electric car that was driving in front of you in the tunnel has caught fire.
What do you do?

A. You get out to see if you can help.
B. You drive backwards, fearing the car might explode.
C. You park your car and leave the tunnel through the emergency exit.
e:
LESSON 29
In case of fire in a tunnel, park the car on the right-hand lane. Leave the keys in the ignition and exit the tunnel through the emergency exit.

q:
What is the maximum speed allowed for the red car on this ordinary road in the Brussels Region?

A. 120 km/h.
B. 90 km/h.
C. 70 km/h.
e:
LESSON 2
On an ordinary road, under normal conditions, the maximum permitted speed in the Walloon Region is 90 km/h.
In the Flemish Region and the Brussels Region, it is 70 km/h.

q:
Are you allowed to overtake the motorcycle on the left at this intersection?

A. Yes.
B. Yes, if you leave sufficient lateral distance.
C. No.
e:
LESSON 16
At intersections where no traffic signs regulate priority, or where this road sign is present and therefore the priority to the right applies, it is forbidden to overtake on the left:
a vehicle with a trailer,
a vehicle with more than two wheels,
or a motor vehicle with two wheels.
A cyclist is not a motor vehicle, so you are allowed to overtake them. You may also overtake a personal mobility device.

q:
Today is 12 February.
What is the car allowed to do at this place?

A. Stop and park.
B. Stop only.
C. Neither stop nor park.
e:
LESSON 26
This traffic sign means that parking is prohibited on this side of the carriageway from the 1st to the 15th of the month.
The additional plate with a downward arrow indicates that the parking prohibition applies up to the sign.
➡️ Beyond the sign, the car may stop and park.

q:
Your car starts to skid.
In which direction should you look?

A. Towards the danger.
B. Towards the direction you intended to go.
e:
LESSON 31
Although braking is probably your first instinct, you should avoid braking too hard. This can cause the wheels to lock and reduce traction.
What should you do instead?
Look in the direction you want to go. Unconsciously, you will steer towards where you are looking. You can imagine what happens if you keep looking at the danger.
Steer in the opposite direction (counter-steer).
Press the clutch and release the accelerator.
